era_context: The early 20th century, dominated by European imperial competition and industrial expansion. The Russo-Japanese War begins, marking a major shift in Asian geopolitics. In Europe, the Entente Cordiale between Britain and France signals a realignment of power. The Ottoman Empire continues its decline. In the Americas, the United States consolidates its regional dominance.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War Begins
events.description: The Russo-Japanese War began on February 8, 1904, with a Japanese surprise attack on the Russian fleet at Port Arthur. The war was fought over control of Manchuria and Korea, both regions of strategic importance to Russia and Japan. Japan's early victories shocked the Western world, as this was the first major military conflict between a European power and an Asian nation. The war would continue until September 1905, with Japan emerging victorious and fundamentally reshaping East Asian geopolitics.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in military records and contemporary sources. The war's start date and major events are well-established.

events.title: Entente Cordiale Signed Between Britain and France
events.description: Britain and France signed the Entente Cordiale on April 8, 1904, resolving colonial disputes and establishing a framework for cooperation. The agreement settled disputes over Egypt, Morocco, and other colonial territories, and marked a significant shift in European power dynamics. The Entente Cordiale laid the foundation for the Triple Entente (with Russia) and represented a realignment against German power. The agreement would prove crucial in shaping alliances during World War I.
key_figures: Edward VII (King of the United Kingdom)

sources.name: Official treaty text and diplomatic records

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records and contemporary sources. The treaty date and terms are well-established.

events.title: Panama Canal Construction Begins
events.description: The United States, under President Theodore Roosevelt, began construction of the Panama Canal on January 22, 1904. The canal was intended to connect the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans, dramatically reducing shipping times and enhancing U.S. strategic power. The project involved massive engineering challenges and the deaths of thousands of workers, many from tropical diseases. The canal was completed in 1914 and became one of the most important strategic and commercial waterways in the world.
key_figures: Lesseps, Ferdinand de (French engineer; earlier attempt)

sources.name: U.S. government records and engineering reports

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in U.S. government records and contemporary sources. The construction start date and major milestones are well-established.

events.title: Herero Uprising in German Southwest Africa
events.description: The Herero people of German Southwest Africa (present-day Namibia) rose up against German colonial rule in January 1904, led by Chief Samuel Maharero. The uprising was triggered by German expropriation of Herero cattle and land. German General Lothar von Trotha responded with a brutal campaign of extermination, driving the Herero into the Omaheke Desert. The genocide killed an estimated 65,000 Herero (about 80% of the population). The Herero Uprising and subsequent genocide represent one of the first genocides of the 20th century.

sources.name: German colonial records and military reports
Oral traditions and later Herero accounts

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in German colonial records and contemporary sources. The uprising's start date and general events are well-established, though casualty figures vary.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War: Battle of the Yellow Sea
events.description: The Battle of the Yellow Sea (August 10, 1904) was a major naval engagement between Russian and Japanese fleets during the Russo-Japanese War. The Japanese fleet, under Admiral Togo, defeated the Russian fleet, further establishing Japanese naval superiority. The battle demonstrated the effectiveness of modern naval tactics and technology and contributed to Japan's overall victory in the war.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in naval records and contemporary sources. The battle date and outcome are well-established.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War: Siege of Port Arthur
events.description: The Siege of Port Arthur (August 1904–January 1905) was a major military engagement during the Russo-Japanese War. Japanese forces besieged the Russian-held fortress of Port Arthur, eventually forcing its surrender on January 2, 1905. The siege was one of the longest and bloodiest of the war, with tens of thousands of casualties on both sides. The fall of Port Arthur demonstrated Japanese military superiority and contributed to Russia's eventual defeat.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in military records and contemporary sources. The siege dates and outcome are well-established.

events.title: St. Louis World's Fair
events.description: The 1904 World's Fair (Louisiana Purchase Exposition) was held in St. Louis from April to December 1904. The fair showcased American technological and cultural achievements and attracted over 19 million visitors. The fair featured exhibits from around the world and included the first modern Olympic Games (held in conjunction with the fair). The fair symbolized American confidence and power at the turn of the 20th century.
key_figures: Roosevelt, Theodore (U.S. President; opened the fair)

sources.name: Official fair records and contemporary newspapers

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records and contemporary sources. The fair dates and major events are well-established.

events.title: 1904 Summer Olympics Held in St. Louis
events.description: The 1904 Summer Olympics were held in St. Louis in conjunction with the World's Fair. The games featured competitions in athletics, swimming, gymnastics, and other sports. The 1904 Olympics were notable for being dominated by American athletes and for the exclusion of most international competitors due to the difficulty of traveling to the United States. The games symbolized American athletic prowess and the growing importance of organized international sports.
key_figures: Coubertin, Pierre de (International Olympic Committee founder)

sources.name: Official Olympic records and contemporary newspapers

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records and contemporary sources. The Olympic dates and events are well-established.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War: Battle of Mukden
events.description: The Battle of Mukden (February–March 1904) was one of the largest land battles of the Russo-Japanese War. Japanese forces under General Oyama defeated Russian forces under General Kuropatkin, forcing the Russians to retreat northward. The battle demonstrated Japanese military superiority and contributed to Russia's eventual defeat in the war. The battle was one of the largest military engagements of the early 20th century.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in military records and contemporary sources. The battle dates and outcome are well-established.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War: Battle of Tsushima
events.description: The Battle of Tsushima (May 27–28, 1905) was a decisive naval battle between the Russian and Japanese fleets during the Russo-Japanese War. The Japanese fleet, under Admiral Togo, decisively defeated the Russian Baltic Fleet, which had sailed around the world to reinforce Russian forces in the Far East. The battle resulted in the destruction of most of the Russian fleet and effectively ended Russian naval power in the Pacific. The battle is considered one of the most significant naval engagements of the early 20th century.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in naval records and contemporary sources. The battle date and outcome are well-established. Note: This battle occurred in May 1905, not 1904, but is included here as a major event of the Russo-Japanese War that began in 1904.

events.title: Qing Dynasty Begins Modernization Reforms
events.description: Following China's defeat in the Boxer Rebellion (1900–1901) and the subsequent foreign occupation of Beijing, the Qing government began a series of modernization reforms in 1904. The reforms included military reorganization, educational reform, and administrative restructuring. The reforms were intended to strengthen China and prevent further foreign encroachment, but they also exposed the limitations of the Qing system and contributed to growing calls for more radical change.

sources.name: Qing imperial edicts and government records
Contemporary Chinese and Western observers' accounts

events.certainty_note: The Qing reforms began gradually in the early 1900s; 1904 is a conventional date for their acceleration, though the exact start date is somewhat arbitrary.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War: Siege of Port Arthur Begins
events.description: The Siege of Port Arthur began in August 1904 when Japanese forces surrounded the Russian-held fortress. The siege would last until January 1905 and would become one of the longest and bloodiest engagements of the Russo-Japanese War. The siege demonstrated the effectiveness of modern siege warfare and artillery tactics.
events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in military records and contemporary sources. The siege start date is well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Tsushima occurred in May 1905, not 1904, though it was a major event of the Russo-Japanese War that began in 1904. The Herero Uprising's death toll is disputed; estimates range from 65,000 to over 100,000, and some scholars debate whether it constitutes a genocide in the modern sense or a brutal colonial war. The Entente Cordiale is sometimes portrayed as a formal military alliance, but it was primarily a colonial agreement with no explicit military commitment; the military alliance developed later. The Panama Canal construction start date is sometimes given as January 22, 1904, but some sources date the beginning of American construction efforts to earlier in 1904 or late 1903.
historiographic_note: 1904 is richly documented in European, American, Russian, and Japanese sources, reflecting the dominance of Western and Japanese archives. The Russo-Japanese War is extensively covered in military records and contemporary sources from all major powers. Events in Africa (Herero Uprising) are documented primarily in German colonial records and later African sources, with indigenous perspectives often underrepresented. The year is heavily shaped by narratives of European imperial competition and technological progress; indigenous perspectives on colonial events are often filtered through colonial historiography. The Herero Uprising is significantly underrepresented in Western historical narratives compared to its significance as one of the first genocides of the 20th century.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of the Yellow Sea was a major engagement within the Russo-Japanese War
The Siege of Port Arthur was a major engagement within the Russo-Japanese War
The Battle of Mukden was a major engagement within the Russo-Japanese War
The 1904 Olympics were held in conjunction with the St. Louis World's Fair
The Russo-Japanese War led to the Treaty of Portsmouth in 1905
Both the Herero Uprising and the Russo-Japanese War occurred in 1904, representing major colonial and imperial conflicts
